minimally invasive surgery for inguinal lymph node dissection was first used by Dargent and he did about ten to fifteen cases laparoscopically but subsequently for whatever reason he stopped doing that this was in early eighties it was then picked up by the urologists and they started doing a Brazilian group started doing laparoscopic inguinal lymphadenectomy for penile cancers It is the urologist actually who have the largest series of inguinal lymph node dissections. But in our hospital, we too are doing it for vulval cancers.

We started with laparoscopic inguinal lymph node dissection, but now we've graduated onto robotic inguinal lymph node dissections. And this is done for very select cases. Preferably, where the inguinal lymph nodes are not palpably enlarged, or if they are palpable, maybe sub-centimeter or something. That's one. Secondly, we make it a point to send the specimen for frozen section. If in the frozen section it is positive, any lymph node is positive, it is mandatory to do an open dissection for them, so that you should not have left behind any lymph nodes.

Basically to reduce the morbidity. All of us know how much morbidity is involved in inguinal lymph node dissection and how the patients have wound dehiscence and necrosis and lymphoria and what have you. So I'll just quickly show you a video of robotic inguinal lymph node dissection and we'll take it from there.

The patient is positioned supine with legs abducted, and the femoral triangle is marked, showing the anterior superior iliac spine and pubic tubercle. The camera port is placed four centimeters below the apex of the femoral triangle, with two robotic ports positioned six centimeters superior medially and laterally from the camera port. The assistant port is visible, used for handling lymph nodes. The robotic system is docked parallel to the limb on the lateral side, with the assistant standing nearby. The camera port and two robotic arms with monopolar and bipolar instruments are shown.

The surgeon creates a pneumoperitoneum, visually resembling a frog's back, by first making a space with a finger. The operator palpates to ensure the skin is not overly thinned and remains in the correct plane above Scarpa's fascia.

That's how it is. See, you can go as, if you're not careful, you can go as close to the skin as possible. This is what we have to avoid. This can cause avascular necrosis of the skin and then the whole purpose is defeated or it can even lead to a puff over here with the instruments.
The monitor displays a retrograde dissection being performed, where the surgeon is dissecting from below upwards along the saphenous vein towards the femoral vessels.

The surgeon clamps large vessels and fulgurates smaller vessels while navigating within the femoral triangle. The sartorius muscle forms the lateral border, and the adductor muscle forms the medial border. An accessory saphenous vein is visible.
The surgeon applies a clip to a large vessel and then cuts it, ensuring secure ligation before proceeding with the dissection.
The assistant uses a clip from the assistant port to secure the vessels, visible within the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the named branches originating from the inguinal ligament, including the superficial external pudendal, superficial circumflex iliac, and the medial and lateral branches of the superficial iliac artery.

All the time you have to make sure that you are in the right plane, you are not too close to the skin. So the trick is to keep the softness at the base. You spot the softness and keep the softness at the base of your dissection.

The surgeon identifies and exposes the superficial vessels, including the superficial circumflex iliac and the superficial branch of the superficial external pudendal, as perforators within the surgical field.
The surgeon separates the fatty tissue, ensuring that lymph nodes are included with the specimen and not left behind in the patient.
So it takes a while to learn that the skin thickness is adequate, not too much and not too less. You have not left any lymph nodes behind and that the skin is not too thin for it to get necrosed.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to perform precise dissection around the lingual and lymph node areas, highlighting the instrument's suitability over a fenestrated bipolar for this task.
We have clipped all the vessels. You have to be very careful. The pressure is generally kept at about eight mm or so. It's a small confined space, so you cannot have the pressure for more than that.
The surgeon carefully dissects along the border of the muscle, removing all visible fibro fatty tissue while being mindful of the lighting conditions.
A small branch of a vessel is visible, arising from the femoral artery, with noticeable pulsations.

And for the vulva, we know that there's no such thing as doing only superficial lymph nodes. We always do the deep femorals as well unlike what they do for penile cancers over there they first do the superficial inguinal lymph nodes they send it for frozen if positive only then do they dissect out the deep inguinal nodes whereas for vulval cancers we always do both sets of nodes because some for some patients the vulva drains directly into the deep nodes. Certain areas of the vulva drain directly into the deep nodes and in about as high as about seven to eight percent of the cases you might have a deep node which is positive and the superficial inguinal nodes might be negative. So, therefore, it is important that we dissect out the deep inguinal lymph nodes as well.
The surgeon approaches the saphenous vein during the dissection, carefully attempting to preserve it while performing an inguinal lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon clips the saphenous vein as it opens into the femoral vein and proceeds to cut it off. A gauze is placed over the area for hemostasis and to clear the surgical field, which can later be removed through one of the ports.
The inguinal ligament is visible above, marking the boundary of the surgical field. The dissection within the inguinal triangle is nearly complete.

The surgeon identifies the accessory saphenous vein and applies a clip, which appears to be slipping. A suture is used to secure the vein, demonstrating the ease of suturing in robotic surgery compared to laparoscopic techniques.
The surgical field is fully exposed, showing the femoral region with completed dissection.
The surgeon reduces the pressure to check for any remaining bleeding before concluding the surgery. The specimen is extracted through the system port, marking the completion of the procedure.
